Republic of North Macedonia: North Macedonia, officially the Republic of North Macedonia, is a landlocked country in Southeast Europe. It shares land borders with Kosovo to the northwest, Serbia to the north, Bulgaria to the east, Greece to the south, and Albania to the west. It constitutes approximately the northern third of the larger geographical region of Macedonia. Skopje, the capital and largest city, is home to a quarter of the country's population. The majority of the residents are ethnic Macedonians, a South Slavic people. Albanians form a significant minority at around 25 percent, followed by Turks, Romani, Serbs, Bosniaks, and Aromanians.
Alexander the Great: Alexander III of Macedon, most commonly known as Alexander the Great, was king of the ancient Greek kingdom of Macedon. Born in Pella, Macedon, Alexander was tutored by Aristotle until the age of 16. By the age of 30, he had created one of the largest empires in history, stretching from Greece to northwestern India. He was undefeated in battle and is widely considered one of history's most successful military commanders.
Ss. Cyril and Methodius University of Sk: The Ss. Cyril and Methodius University in Skopje is a public university in North Macedonia. It is the largest and oldest public university in the country. The university was founded in 1949. It is named after Saints Cyril and Methodius. The university includes many faculties and institutes and is located in Skopje.
Skopje: Skopje is the capital and largest city of North Macedonia. It is the political, cultural, economic, and academic center of the country. It lies on the upper course of the Vardar River and is a major center in the Balkans. The city has a long history, with roots in antiquity as the Roman settlement of Scupi. Skopje suffered a devastating earthquake in 1963 and was rebuilt with international assistance.
Macedonian language: The Macedonian language is a South Slavic language spoken as a first language by around two million people, principally in North Macedonia and the Macedonian diaspora. It is the official language of North Macedonia. Standard Macedonian was codified in 1945. Macedonian uses the Cyrillic alphabet and is closely related to Bulgarian.
Lake Ohrid: Lake Ohrid is a lake which straddles the mountainous border between the southwestern part of North Macedonia and eastern Albania. It is one of Europe's deepest and oldest lakes. The lake preserves a unique aquatic ecosystem with more than 200 endemic species. Lake Ohrid and the city of Ohrid are recognized as UNESCO World Heritage Sites.
Samuel of Macedonia: Samuel was a medieval ruler who established a powerful Slavic empire in the Macedonian region. He made Ohrid his capital and cultural center, in present-day North Macedonia. Samuel ruled over a large territory in the western Balkans. His empire is considered a significant part of Macedonian medieval history. His army suffered a major defeat at the Battle of Kleidion in 1014 against Emperor Basil II of the Byzantine Empire.
Cyril and Methodius: Saints Cyril and Methodius were Byzantine Christian theologians and missionaries. They are credited with creating the Glagolitic alphabet, the first alphabet used to transcribe Old Church Slavonic. Their disciples later developed the Cyrillic alphabet. They made a major contribution to the cultural development of Slavic peoples.
Byzantine Empire: The Byzantine Empire, also known as the Eastern Roman Empire, was the continuation of the Roman Empire in its eastern provinces during Late Antiquity and the Middle Ages. Its capital was Constantinople. The empire played a major role in the spread of Christianity and influenced the development of Slavic cultures.
Ohrid: Ohrid is a city on the shore of Lake Ohrid in North Macedonia. It is one of the oldest human settlements in Europe. Ohrid is known for its churches, monasteries, and historical monuments. The city and lake area are part of the UNESCO World Heritage List.
